title: Defending the Network
concept: Adversarial training, where a model is trained on active adversarial examples, is the most robust defense against attacks.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between FGSM and PGD?

options.text: FGSM is a single-step attack; PGD is a multi-step iterative attack
FGSM works offline; PGD requires active cloud servers
FGSM is only for classification; PGD is only for segmentation

exercises.explain: FGSM is fast and single-step, while PGD is iterative and much stronger.

cards.title: Immunizing the AI
cards.body: How do you protect a model against attacks? You can't block every hacker. The best solution is like a vaccine: you show the model adversarial attacks during training, teaching it to recognize the noise and ignore it!

cards.title: Adversarial Training
cards.body: Adversarial training is the process of generating adversarial examples (using PGD) during every step of training and adding them to the training batches. The model learns to classify both clean and attacked images correctly.

cards.title: A martial arts sparring partner
cards.body: If you only practice martial arts against partners who tell you exactly where they will punch, you will freeze in a real fight. Adversarial training is like sparring with a partner who actively tries to find your weak spots, making you robust.

cards.title: Training with PGD examples
cards.body: A banking app trains their check scanner. The training script runs PGD on each batch, creating attacked checks, and trains the weights on both versions. The final model is immune to standard digital fraud attacks.

cards.title: The robustness trade-off
cards.body: You might think adversarial training is a free upgrade. In reality, there is a trade-off: models trained on adversarial examples usually experience a 2-5% drop in accuracy on clean, normal images.

left.text:
100% clean images
High accuracy on clean data
0% robustness to attacks
Fast training time

right.text:
Clean + PGD-attacked images
Slightly lower clean accuracy
High robustness to attacks
3x slower training time

exercises.prompt: What is the most effective known method for defending neural networks against adversarial attacks?

options.text: Adversarial training (training on active adversarial examples)
Increasing the JPEG compression of input images
Converting all model weights to INT8 precision
Removing all skip connections from the model graph

exercises.explain: Adversarial training teaches the model weights to resist perturbations by exposing them during training.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Adversarial training usually increases a model's accuracy on clean, normal images.
exercises.explain: False. There is a trade-off: defending the model slightly reduces its peak performance on clean datasets.

exercises.sentence: Exposing a model to simulated attacks during training acts like a vaccine, creating adversarial ___.
exercises.explain: Robustness is the model's ability to maintain correct predictions despite input perturbations.

exercises.prompt: Why does adversarial training slow down the overall training process?

options.text: The training loop must run backpropagation multiple times per batch to generate PGD attacks
It requires downloading files from cloud servers during training
It forces the model to run on the CPU only

exercises.explain: Generating PGD attacks is a multi-step gradient process, multiplying the calculations required for each training step.

exercises.prompt: You are deploying a facial recognition entry system for a secure facility. You want to make sure the model cannot be fooled by digital noise injected into the camera stream. What training setup should you use?

options.text: Generate PGD perturbations during training and train the model on a mix of clean and perturbed images.
Train the model solely on high-resolution clean photos.
Apply post-training quantization to INT8 without retraining.
Apply vertical graph fusion to the model layers.

exercises.explain: Adversarial training using PGD is the industry-standard way to immunize facial recognition networks against digital perturbation attacks.
